summary: Jeff Atwood explores the 'Last Responsible Moment' principle from Lean Software Development, arguing that delaying decisions until you have maximum information leads to better outcomes. He distinguishes this strategic delay from mere procrastination, calling it 'inspired laziness' that serves as a risk avoidance strategy. The post connects this concept to YAGNI (You Ain't Gonna Need It) and argues against our natural tendency to over-prepare. Using his own workshop full of unused tools as an example, Atwood illustrates how premature commitments waste resources and time. He concludes that in the rapidly changing world of software, deciding too early can be more dangerous than deciding too late.
key_points: Decisions made too early in a project carry enormous risk and often result in throwaway work or crippling long-term consequences
The Last Responsible Moment is the point at which failing to decide eliminates an important alternative - not procrastination but strategic delay
Having the courage to say 'I don't know, but we're working on it' is often the best decision early in a project
Humans have a natural tendency to prepare for unlikely future needs, wasting resources on things they'll never use
In software's rapidly changing environment, premature decisions are arguably more dangerous than late ones
money_quotes: Making decisions at the Last Responsible Moment isn't procrastination; it's inspired laziness.
Decisions made too early in a project are hugely risky.
Often, having the guts to say 'I don't know' is your best decision.
The key is to make decisions as late as you can responsibly wait because that is the point at which you have the most information on which to base the decision.
Early decisions often result in work that has to be thrown away.
Be prepared. But for tomorrow, not next year.
Deciding too late is dangerous, but deciding too early in the rapidly changing world of software development is arguably even more dangerous.
key_insight: Strategic delay of decisions until the last responsible moment isn't procrastination—it's a risk management technique that ensures you're working with maximum information rather than costly assumptions.
